title: Settings for All Character Sets
text: Settings for All Character Sets The .ini and .asn settings are used for all character sets. Table 1. Settings in .ini file Setting Description nls Specify the character set that is supported by the current Uniface installation. Profile Specify the display profile characters in widgets. (Windows only) [printer] and [screen] nls Some GUI settings are dependent on the character set. For example, a Simplified Chinese Uniface version, has setting HyperLabel=Arial,Simplified Chinese,8,underline Uniface is character set independent. It's internal character set is UTF-8, but it can be run in any Uniface supported character sets environment. Such independence is achieved by means of translation tables. A translation table defines a mapping between the Uniface internal character set and a Uniface supported character set. For example, the Euro sign € is coded as x20AC (Unicode) in Uniface internally, and as x80 in character set CP1252. In the Uniface translation table CP1252, there is an entry that maps x20AC to x80 and vice versa. When Uniface writes € into a file that is stored as CP1252 code, Uniface sends code x80 to the file. Table 2. Translation Tables in Assignment File Setting Description $sys_charset Specify a character set used to communicate with the operating system. $def_charset Specify a character set used to communicate with the database. $keyboard Specify a translation table used for entering characters. display Specify a translation table used for displaying characters. $LANGUAGE Uniface installation program initializes the $LANGUAGE with the language abbreviation of the installed character set in the usys.asn file. For example, if you selected Japanese during the installation, $LANGUAGE = JPN . However, you can change this setting according to the library and global objects used in your application. For more information, see language . You can define multiple language versions of global objects in a library. $LANGUAGE is used together with $VARIATION to determine which language version of the global objects are used in your application. For more information, see Libraries of Runtime Objects and $variation . [META_ LANGUAGE ] In earlier Uniface versions, its internal character set was the meta character set. From version 9, Unicode becomes its internal character set. For the compatibility reason, the meta character set is still used. These result in some problems. This assignment section is used to define your own character mapping to solve the problems. For more information, see Unicode Compatibility Issues . DEFAULTENCODING For a web application, the browser should be able to display the specific language. Make sure that the browser's Encoding view is switched to the correct language. To do so, set the DEFAULTENCODING parameter. For more information, see DEFAULTENCODING . Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
